title: SMT2 Backend Treats Non-POT-Sized Memories as next higher POT
body:
## Steps to reproduce the issue

This is a simplified version of `syncram_9_4.v` from https://github.com/YosysHQ/yosys-bench/tree/master/verilog/benchmarks_small/ram:

```
module syncram_9_4 (
    clk,
    addr_in,
    data_out    
);

input clk;
input [8:0] addr_in;
output reg [3:0] data_out;

reg [3:0] mem [0:8];

always @(posedge clk)
begin
  data_out <= mem[addr_in];
end

endmodule
```

Reading, creating a Miter circuit comparing two instances of this module and writing to SMT2 with this Yosys script:

```
read_verilog syncram_9_4.v
miter -equiv -make_assert syncram_9_4 syncram_9_4 miter
prep -top miter
setundef -zero -init -undriven -params
write_smt2 miter.smt2
```
Note that `setundef` adds zero-initialization to all memory lines.

Now running through `yosys-smtbmc`:

```
yosys-smtbmc -s boolector --dump-vlogtb tb.v miter.smt2
```

For some reason, `yices` does not like this smt2 file (`Unexpected EOF response from solver.`), so I am using boolector. If I am doing anything wrong, please tell me :)

## Expected behavior

The assertions should pass.

## Actual behavior

```
##   0:00:00  BMC failed!
##   0:00:00  Assert failed in miter: $auto$miter.cc:233:create_miter_equiv$5
##   0:00:00  Status: FAILED (!)
```

## Analysis
When writing the memory to SMT2, its actual size is never written, only its number of address bits. This can be seen when changing the memory's size in the original source, e.g. to `reg [4-1:0] mem [0:9];`. The diff between the two smt2 files is:

```
--- miter_9.smt2	2020-12-01 16:07:26.124944308 +0100
+++ miter_10.smt2	2020-12-01 16:07:39.286427423 +0100
@@ -32,6 +32,7 @@
   (= (bvand (select (|syncram_9_4#3#0| state) #b0110) #b1111) #b0000) ; mem[6]
   (= (bvand (select (|syncram_9_4#3#0| state) #b0111) #b1111) #b0000) ; mem[7]
   (= (bvand (select (|syncram_9_4#3#0| state) #b1000) #b1111) #b0000) ; mem[8]
+  (= (bvand (select (|syncram_9_4#3#0| state) #b1001) #b1111) #b0000) ; mem[9]
 ))
 (define-fun |syncram_9_4_h| ((state |syncram_9_4_s|)) Bool true)
 (define-fun |syncram_9_4_t| ((state |syncram_9_4_s|) (next_state |syncram_9_4_s|)) Bool (and
```
The added line is due to `setundef` initializing the new memory location. As both a 9-entry and a 10-entry memory have an address width of 4 bits, the actual memory did not change.

The memory's size is therefore rounded up to the next power of two. It no longer has 9 lines, but 16. As these lines do not exist in the design, they are not initialized by `setundef`.  The solver is free to choose any initialization for them, as can be seen in the generated testbench:
```
    UUT.gate.mem[4'b1010] = 4'b0111;
    UUT.gold.mem[4'b1010] = 4'b1000;
```
As the solver was free to choose different initializations, the assertion failed.
code_blocks:
read_verilog syncram_9_4.v
miter -equiv -make_assert syncram_9_4 syncram_9_4 miter
prep -top miter
setundef -zero -init -undriven -params
write_smt2 miter.smt2

##   0:00:00  BMC failed!
##   0:00:00  Assert failed in miter: $auto$miter.cc:233:create_miter_equiv$5
##   0:00:00  Status: FAILED (!)
